title: Mission 04 — Night Passenger Pressure
summary: A night cross-country with passenger expectations, black-hole visual risk, and increasing pressure to keep the schedule. This mission is about saying no before the situation owns you.

weather.summary: Night VFR with adequate weather, but reduced visual cues, passenger pressure, and a long dark stretch westbound increase the risk picture.

notams: Several towers west of Shelbyville are unlit above surrounding terrain.

personalMinimums: Night passenger carrying only if current and well-rested
Passenger schedule is never the deciding factor

events.title: Night Passenger Currency
events.prompt: You are legal and current, but slightly tired after a long day. What is the best self-assessment?

options.label: Use IMSAFE honestly and be willing to delay if fatigue is affecting judgment or performance.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Night flights increase workload, so fatigue deserves extra respect.
consequence.commMessage: Night flight only if IMSAFE is honestly green.

options.label: Go because legal currency automatically means you are fit to go.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect mindset. Currency is a legal standard, not proof of readiness.

options.label: Launch anyway and just avoid mentioning the fatigue concern.

consequence.feedback: Poor ADM. Unacknowledged fatigue tends to appear when the workload spikes.

events.title: Passenger Expectations
events.prompt: Your passenger says they absolutely must arrive tonight. What is the best response?

options.label: Set the expectation now that the flight only happens if risk remains acceptable all the way through landing.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Boundaries set early reduce pressure later in the flight.
consequence.commMessage: Passenger brief complete: schedule does not override safety.

options.label: Promise that you’ll get them there unless something really extreme happens.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. Promising completion creates exactly the external pressure ADM is meant to prevent.

options.label: Laugh it off and avoid discussing the possibility of a delay or diversion.

consequence.feedback: Weak CRM. The passenger should understand that diversion or cancellation is always on the table.

events.title: Night Reserve Margin
events.prompt: What fuel strategy is best for a night mission with possible delay vectors or a precautionary return?

options.label: Carry 62 gallons to maintain a generous reserve beyond the legal night minimum.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Night and passenger pressure justify extra practical reserve.

options.label: Take only what you need plus 45 minutes because the regs say that is enough.

consequence.feedback: Poor planning. Legal minimums are not an ideal target for a night passenger mission.

options.label: Top off regardless of passenger comfort, runway length, or performance impact.

consequence.feedback: Not terrible, but the best answer balances reserve with the rest of the mission profile.

events.title: Sparse Lights Ahead
events.prompt: Approach to Louisville places you over a dark area with minimal visual references. What is the best technique adjustment?

options.label: Cross-check instruments and maintain a stabilized profile instead of trusting visual illusions alone.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Black-hole conditions demand disciplined instrument cross-checking even in VFR.

options.label: Descend based on how low the runway lights look from the windshield.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. Visual illusion risk is exactly why you do not trust outside cues alone here.

options.label: Increase speed a little so you spend less time over the dark area.

consequence.feedback: Poor technique. Rushing a dark approach reduces your margin further.

events.title: Passenger Anxiety in Cruise
events.prompt: Your passenger becomes anxious and asks you to descend lower because they can’t see much outside. What is the best response?

options.label: Reassure them, explain the plan, and maintain the safer cruise profile rather than descending for comfort.

consequence.feedback: Correct. Manage the passenger, but do not let their anxiety create a poorer altitude choice.
consequence.commMessage: Maintaining safer altitude despite passenger request to descend.

options.label: Descend below your planned altitude so the passenger feels better.

consequence.feedback: Weak ADM. Descending at night for comfort is a poor trade unless it clearly improves safety.

options.label: Tell them to be quiet and stop talking during the flight.

consequence.feedback: Not ideal CRM. You should keep command authority without creating unnecessary tension.

events.title: Stabilized or Divert
events.prompt: You realize you are not stable by your desired gate on final. What is the best action?

options.label: Go around early, reset, and if needed divert rather than force the night landing.

consequence.feedback: Correct. A go-around or diversion is always better than salvaging an unstable night approach.
consequence.commMessage: Tower, Skylane 734JP going around to re-stabilize.

options.label: Force the landing because you are close and the passenger wants to be done.

consequence.feedback: Incorrect. Pressing an unstable night landing is a classic bad-outcome setup.

options.label: Keep making large corrections and hope it comes together by the threshold.

consequence.feedback: Weak. Large late corrections are the signal to abandon the approach, not continue.
